topic: Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill
description: Monday 30 March 2026 | Education and Workforce subcommittee A Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill New Zealand Council of Trade Unions Te Kauae Kaimahi - Sandra Grey, President - Cory Bourne, Team Leader Policy Cultureshift NZ Ltd - Allan Halse, Director New Zealand Professional Firefighters Union - Wattie Watson, National Secretary GM Safety Forum - Max Riley - Rob Mair HS Education Forum - Dave Moore - Mia Alunno Jane Peymen New Zealand Occupational Hygiene Society (NZOHS) - Miriska Gerber, President - Nadine Barnard Zoran Rakovic Stand Up youth union movement - Xavier Walsh, Co-Convenor New Zealand Plant Producers - Matthew Dolan Rail Maritime Transport Union - Karen Fletcher Lily Hall Butcher Workdek NZ Ltd - Dave Simmons, Director New Zealand Institute of Safety Management (NZISM) - Robyn Bennett, Chair - Jeff Sissons, CEO - Mike Cosman, Law Reform Working Group Chair
descriptionSummary: Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill. New Zealand Council of Trade Unions Te Kauae Kaimahi. Sandra Grey, President

chunks.text: Our committee members who don't know about the CTU, we represent 32 affiliated unions across almost every sector of the New Zealand economy. 340,000 union members are affiliated through their unions, making us probably the largest democratic organization in New Zealand, if not one of the largest. We do want to start by acknowledging Te Tiriti o Waitangi as the founding document of Aotearoa, and we do our work through Te Rūnanga Ó Ngā Kaimahi Māori, which is the Māori arm of Te Kāwai Kaimahi, and they represent around 60,000 Māori workers.

I want to begin by saying that the CTU supports the submissions of our affiliated trade unions. Those submissions that you've been hearing already and the ones to come will provide you as a select committee with critical insight into how the proposed changes to our health and safety legislation will significantly and negatively impact on workers and their whānau. The CTU does not support this bill progressing, and we ask that you do not move it into the second reading.

We want to take that you've read the submission and not go into the substantive detail, but we do want to say that that substantive detail, the substantive concerns we have raised in our written submission, are not only in line with other worker submissions, but they are broadly in line with many industry associations'

concerns and the concerns of professionals in the health and safety space and of academics who work in this area. So there seems now to be quite a united voice coming to this committee and saying that these changes are neither appropriate nor needed, and that they will in fact be dangerous for workers.

Trade unions act daily in the space of health and safety in trying to protect the safety of workers, and we advocate strongly to make sure that we have safe practice at work. Our work as a council of trade unions includes advocating both nationally and internationally to ensure that the collective voice of working New Zealanders is heard on the matters of health and safety, both daily in their workplaces, but in forums like this, in select committees and international forums.

And I do want to point out that we don't just speak for union members, that actually the work we do supports all working people, whether in union or not. This bill has to be seen in the context of a range of recent legislative changes that are being pushed through by this government, which are driven not by good evidence, not by good information, but in fact are just being driven by ideology. The bill sits in the context of a long rat sheet of anti-worker legislation from the past few months and longer, including the

reintroduction of 90-day trials and the changes to the Employment Relations Amendment Act, which will substantially, for most workers, abolish their remedies when they are unjustifiably dismissed, and also that Employment Relations Act will allow employers to contract out of their employment, or contract out employment rights, I should say.

Then there is the scrapping of the fair pay agreements and pay equity claims affecting hundreds and thousands of workers who really need a lift in their incomes. These changes represent significant, significant backward steps with regard work rights, and they undermine the system that oversees and protects the rights of workers.

And this bill currently before you as a select committee should be viewed in relation to all those other law changes as it and the other law changes are making workers more vulnerable and increasing insecurity in our workplaces. And when that comes to the health and safety of workers, we already have a really terrible, terrible record as a country.

And I'm sure you know these figures, but in honor of those workers who have lost their lives at work, I want to repeat that about 18 workers a week are killed because of work. And every 15 minutes, a worker suffers an injury that requires more than a week off work. And every year we lose nearly 900 New Zealanders from work-related health illnesses and injuries. So our record is appalling. And this reform, in fact, winds back the last significant reform and the enacting of the Health and Safety at Work Act, and we need to remember the history of why we have the Health and Safety at

Work Act, and that is the terrible disaster at Pike River, where 29 workers were killed because of serious health and safety failures. We all agreed at that time New Zealand had to do something about this, had to make sure we never saw another Pike River again, and we strengthened our health and safety legislation. We are deeply concerned that Parliament, and in particular this government, has already forgotten the lessons we learned from Pike River.

So we have been vocal for a long time, the union movement and the Council of Trade Unions, about improving support for health and safety at work, to give workers strong rights to be healthy and safe and to have a voice in workplace health and safety systems. This bill takes us in absolutely the wrong direction. The changes proposed are regressive and actively weaken the laws and systems designed to keep workers safe and healthy. Passing this bill into law sends a messageTo too many people that harming workers is now acceptable. And I'll just pass over to Corey,

who will just talk through the headlines of our submission. Thank you, Sandra. Yeah, as Sandra mentioned, don't want to get into too much detail, take it as read, but just to highlight some of our key concerns and recommendations from the submission. We do not think that the changes to the critical risk framework align with the proportional risk-based health and safety system, and we don't think they actually meet the stated objectives of the bill itself either.

We think the small PCBU carve-outs create an unjustifiable two-tier health and safety system. Those should be discarded. The health and safety of workers, that doesn't depend on the number of people employed in a business. We think the ACOPS safe harbor provisions will disincentivize continuous improvement, and that will constrain regulatory enforcement in those spaces. Those parts of the bill should not proceed.

And yeah, we think the safe harbor status from those ACOPS changes will have serious unintended consequences and impact on regulator activity. And just finally, we have some serious concerns regarding these changes in terms of our international conventions, commitments, and agreements. We see that a lot of the regressive policy in this bill goes back against the core conventions, the International Labor Organization, conventions 155 and 187, which are both core fundamental conventions at the ILO.

And there are some key trade agreement provisions that expressly prohibit parties from weakening or reducing the levels of protection under labor laws, and we see that this bill is doing that on the basis of trying to cut compliance costs and business activity. Thank you.

Yeah. Thank you very much. We don't appear to have any questions, but that is not a reflection of your submission at all. It's very, very detailed, and in fact, probably why we have none. So thank you very much for your contribution to this process. We do appreciate it. Thank you. Wonderful. We'll move on now to our next submitter, Culture Shift. We have Alan Halse.

Alan, sorry if I have pronounced your surname incorrectly. No. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions, and so please use that as you wish. Over to you. Thank you. And I'd like to thank the committee for granting time today.

Now, I go back probably longer than most of the CTU members. I was a union chairman in Auckland in 1977. So I've been around a long way, but I've gone down a different pathway from the unions over the last 12 years, and I work in the area of psychosocial harm, so in the area of workplace bullying. So, our submissions aren't so much about the relevance of the Amendment Act in terms of the physical harm, although I do agree with a lot of the comments of the Council of

Trade Unions that there is a real risk in the workplace these days. It's greater than I've ever seen in my 56 years of work, and I've worked both in physical work and basically office-based. So we see this Amendment Bill as an opportunity to extend the physical harm to include bullying as a health and safety concern.

And we believe in doing so, that that would signal that government acknowledges the problem of bullying and takes it seriously. We're second worst in the developed world for workplace bullying, and recent figures from Human Rights Commission talk about bullying costing 1.34 billion annually, and they talk about figures as high as 39% of people being bullied.

So when the Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 was passed, and it was passed on the 4th of April 2016, the big difference was they altered in the act the definition of health. So the definition of health now means physical and mental health. Now, when that happened, there was expectations that WorkSafe in particular would start dealing with mental health issues.

So every section of the act that deals with physical health also deals with mental health. Now, at the time it was implemented, and on the same day, 4th of April 2016, prominent constitutional lawyer May Chen of Chen Palmer, said in an opinion piece that was published in The Hill the same day, that mental health in workplace is the new frontier for health and safety.

Now, that's what everybody expected. That's what we expected dealing in the area of workplace bullying. And the idea was that we look beyond physical harm into the area where we're dealing with other issues that include the psychosocial risks, that include workload, job design, and bullying.

And there was an expectation that employers would use tools like ISO to identify hazards and implement controls before harm occurredAnd there was also the expectation that employers and company leaders would act as role models, training supervisors to recognize the stress, burnout, and early warning signs.

Now, utilizing structures that promoted a positive culture, support running to work after mental health struggles, and fostering an environment where employees feel valuable. Now, laws in various jurisdictions, including New Zealand, mandate that employers' duties include mental health and safety, not just physical safety. Now, one of the things that we know from the work that we do, that when somebody is working under mental health pressure, and I'll give an example of

a case where somebody's working in a physical job, say in forestry, where they're cutting down trees and doing that kind of work. They've worked long hours. They're under stress. They have been working for 10, 12 hours. They want to go home.

They say to their employer, "Look, I'm physically exhausted. I'm mentally exhausted. I need to leave." They're threatened with dismissal, and that's bullying again. And they continue to work and suffer an accident. So the psychosocial harm can also influence the physical harm.

Now, Mai Chen highlighted these issues, noting that mental health is as critical as physical safety in modern workplaces. And proactive management improves retention and organizational performance while mitigating legal and health risks. Now, I've been working in this area with my company, Culture Shift New Zealand, for 12 years.

We've represented more than 1,400 workers, and of those 1,400 workers, and we know because we've surveyed them, 87%, by the time they engage our services, are suffering from anxiety, depression, or have PTSD. 50% of them have considered suicide, 9% have attempted suicide. So we have dealt with about 100 workers who have attempted suicide because of what's occurred in their workplace.

Now, this should never happen. Now, we offer hope, but we've tried getting WorkSafe New Zealand to prosecute employers where serious psychological harm has occurred. We've given them cases where we've got the evidence. We've given them cases where there's been suicides, and we've got the suicide notes and the death certificate. WorkSafe New Zealand, New Zealand's health and safety regulator, has not prosecuted a single case, not even one. So the act itself

allows that to happen. Now, in addition to talking about the bullying, and the bullying is a big part of why they attempt suicide, because they've been undermined. The actual act of bullying is about being, I guess, publicly humiliated, isolated, and a whole lot of other things.

So this is an opportunity to understand that the act allows it to happen already. It doesn't need significant change, but there has to be emphasis from Parliament to include bullying and perhaps a definition in the act about what bullying is.

I could talk about this topic forever. It's my favorite topic, and it frustrates me. But I thought I'd end there in case there are any questions from the subcommittee. And I once again thank you for the opportunity to speak. Thank you, Alan. It is very clear that you're passionate about this, so we appreciate that. We do have a question from Dan Rosewarne, so we've got just enough time for that.

Thank you, Chair, and thanks, Alan, for your contribution there. One of the areas I'm concerned about in this bill is the potential unintended impact on workplace safety culture, particularly around the reporting of near misses, and in some workplaces, employees who wish to raise concerns around near misses can be the subject of workplace bullying or harassment. Now, in my experience, near-miss reporting is one of the most effective early warning indicators that we do have, and it allows risks to be identified and addressed

before that actual harm occurs, and you mentioned that before. But what constitutes a near miss is inherently subjective and heavily influenced by that workplace culture. And my question to you is, how do you assess the likely impact of this bill on near-miss reporting behaviors, and what safeguards are needed in this bill, or do you think that are necessary to ensure that near misses continue to be identified and acted on across all workplaces?

And thank you for the question, Dan. I don't believe this amendment is going to help. I haven't seen anything that will help. I'm actually currently in a mediation in Auckland, and I'm representing somebody that's a health and safety rep that got victimized. So when it comes to raising concerns, in our role, we see health and safety reps, health and safety advisors, and health and safety managers being victimized by their employer when they raise concerns, and that's a real risk that I see. So there does need to be that level of protection because people aren't

raising concerns for fear of retaliation and victimization. Thank you very much. That's all the time we have with you today, Alan. So thank you for being part of this process. We do appreciate it. Thank you. Appreciate the time. Thank you all. Thank you. We will move on now to the New Zealand Professional Firefighters Union. We've got Wattie Watson joining us.

Wattie, hopefully you're online, you can hear us. Here we are. If you could turn your camera on and your audio on. Morena. Morena. We have you for 10 minutes, and that does include time for questions, and I'll leave that to you how you utilize it.

And thank you very much. Thank you, Katie. I'm Wattie Watson. I'm the National Secretary of the New Zealand Professional Firefighters Union, and we represent almost 100% of all of New Zealand's career firefighters. We also represent almost 100% of the, uh, fire dispatchers when you call 111 emergency and seek fire.

And we represent more than 85% of everyone employed by FENZ, including those working in risk reduction in health, safety, and wellbeing. I understand that you hopefully have had some time. There's so many submissions. I really do feel for select committees at times. The level of work is huge. But I just want to start by really touching on what the supposed objectives of this bill are, and the first one was to reduce unnecessary compliance costs. Now, what is the cost?

What is the cost of a life? What is the cost of injury? And what is the cost of illness, particularly when it is avoidable, or where proper processes in the workplace would've identified those risks and dealt with it? And, what we often see is a complaint about red tape where there is no actually cost-benefit analysis done. The reality is, employers, regardless of the economic or global environment that we're in, even when we're in good times, employers and businesses are obviously trying to find savings and trying to be as profitable as they can, including

governments and public service. If we do not have strong, robust requirements in law that are punishable, and there are achievable mechanisms to hold people to account, we will not have safe workplaces. The CTU also already demonstrated this morning how dangerous our workplaces are, and by weakening... These laws need strengthening, not weakening.

And we will only see, increasingly workers subjected to unsafe workplaces and very little that they can do about it. The trying to carve out all the small employers is the wording. The current wording is very, very weak and broad. Basically, it says where the employer expects to only have X amount of workers with a nine months average over the year.

We've already had just an attack on what the definition of employer and contractor is. We're yet to see the impact on the workplaces as well. If an employer can regulate how it records who's doing what type of work in the workplace to avoid further obligations in health and safety, there will be employers that do that, and I think that we really need to think seriously about this. The focus on critical risks, critical risks are probably actually sometimes the most identifiable risks, but all risks need to be identified and then appropriately

mitigated to the level of risk. Now, for us, for our membership in particular, for the firefighters, they work for Foreign and Emergency New Zealand, so they don't come under a PCBU that would necessarily be able to avoid the requirements under this act.

However, the changes to the PCBU officers will impact our members as well. So the weakening of the requirements and the obligations, and quite frankly, the stick or the penalty if they don't undertake the obligations seriously will impact on our members. But in particular, my submission was more about what will happen to the firefighters in particular as they respond.

And there's this kind of misunderstanding in New Zealand that the big dangerous fires are the big petrol chemical fires, or the hazardous substance fires. In actual fact, most New Zealand buildings are extremely toxic due to the level of human-made products in construction and in furnishings, and increasing in plastics.

But this act, what this act will do is just a snapshot. Many of New Zealand's-Recycling, which are really basically hoarding of plastics, are done in small industry sites that are dotted around, not remote areas anymore. They can be in quite high industry or commercial areas.

Now, on that site, there might be only one or two workers. Trucks come in and out, drop the stuff. There'll be a worker or two that might be working there, but they would not be covered by this legislation. We already have massive gaps in across the legislation that requires people to properly record the nature of the products or the chemicals or the plastic, whatever it is, on their work site.

We already have huge gaps in that. We have huge gaps in requiring people to have proper evacuation plans, to have fire safety systems, to have, for example, sprinkler systems, to have water available for firefighters, to have space so a fire truck can come up and fight the fire. This, in a combination, we're in a cycle of removing all the necessary protections for our communities, but also for the firefighters that are fighting those fires.

Now, to reduce stuff to critical risks and to only look at Schedule 2 is to really only not even take the tip of the iceberg of what the risks are facing. So we are urging this committee to listen to all the wonderful submissions that have been held and really request you prevent this bill going any further.

Thank you very much, Wadih. Appreciate your submission. We have a question from Dan Rosewarne. Thanks for that submission. I just want to touch on an issue that's particularly relevant for emergency services, and that's the flow of safety-critical information from the front line. Now, in high-risk environments, from my experience, near-miss reporting and open communication are vital, not just for the workforce involved, but for public safety more broadly.

However, in more hierarchical organizations, there can sometimes be a real or perceived constraint on what frontline personnel can actually say, particularly around where operational, reputational, or leadership pressures are present. And my concern is that this bill creates ambiguity around reporting expectations, and it may further discourage the open reporting of near misses and emerging risks from those on the front line. And I just wanted to know your perspective.

Do firefighters feel able to freely report near misses and safety concerns? And how might the changes proposed in this bill affect that? So they do have a system called a Safe at Work, basically app, where they can go in and report that. But what we have seen, even under the current Health and Safety at Work Amendment bill, Act, sorry, not the bill, is that FENZ is increasingly moving away from the ways in which they would inquire into those notifications. So there's policy currently where if there's a near miss or a minor

injury even, or a significant issue at an incident, there would be a level one or two investigation or an operational review. And we're finding, in the last 18 months, I'm fighting, I'm literally writing to FENZ saying, "We must do a review." And those investigations are not about blame on who's ever at the incident or even the systems.

It's about learning so that we make sure that we are identifying these risks and then doing something about them. And FENZ is increasingly making that really hard to get them across the line. We had one recently where three people were injured at a fire and FENZ is telling me we have not met the threshold for what was a level two investigation.

And that's a real concern for us. And this bill just even takes some of the more basic access we have, where we don't have the fight anymore, because they can say, "Look, it's not a requirement under the Act." We are also finding that the health and safety committees aren't working. They just fall by the by. And there's some that work, but some that don't. And when we ask, "Can you please make sure that there is some reporting going on?" It goes on for months, and they say, "Oh, we've asked, but we haven't..."

Delay. So we've got serious problems already under this Act. We're struggling to find teeth with WorkSafe now. This bill will only remove the little leverage that we have to point to a section and say, "Look, you have to do it under this section."

Thank you very much, Wadih. We've finished with our time with you this morning, but as I said, really appreciate your contribution to the process. Very helpful insight. Thank you. Thank you for your time. All right. We will move on now to the GM Safety Forum. We have got with us Max Riley.

There we go. I'll just unmute myself. Yeah. Great. Thank you. And sorry, Rob, I didn't have you on my list, but we've got Rob Mair as well. Good morning. Good morning. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, that includes time for questions, and I'll hand it over to you. Thank you very much. So good morning to the Chair and members of the committee, and thanks for the opportunity to appear. My name is Max Riley.

So I'm here representing the steering committee of the GMs Health and Safety Forum. My regular role is the head of business resilience for Chorus New Zealand. So Rob Mair is a colleague. He's also representing the steering committee of the GMs Forum, and he's the executive general manager for health, safety, and wellbeing at Ports of Auckland.So, to give some context, the GM Forum represents senior health and safety leaders from New Zealand's businesses with complex operations, extensive contractor and supply chain relationships, and exposure to

those critical risk and high consequence events. To help frame up our submission, our members make the health and safety system in New Zealand work in practice across our businesses. We support reforms that make the system clearer and more consistent, and that truly reduce fatalities and serious harm.

From our business and operational point of view, the system works best when responsibilities are clear, easy to understand and manage, and that expectations are consistent across regulators, businesses, clients, and subcontractors. We want to make sure that effort is focused on the risks that can kill and seriously injure people.

We think that unclear signals do not necessarily reduce the burden, and that the system will operate effectively when critical risk is well-defined and applied with consistency. I'm going to touch a little bit on overlapping duties, and Rob will touch a little bit further on critical risk. So, for overlapping duties, our core message is that consistent overlapping duties are essential for serious harm prevention in this complex work environment and arrangement.

They're not unnecessary duplication, they're a safeguard to help guide us through. And that creating the two-tier system just increases red tape for small business. Our critical risks don't change. Risk doesn't really discriminate no matter what size business you are. So, kind of framing that up by the size of your business doesn't matter.

And Rob, did you want to jump in there on critical risk? Thank you, Max. And, yeah, good morning to the members of the committee. As Max indicated, we're supportive of the work to reframe the focus onto critical risk. There's a couple of points that we'd like to make aligned to that. The first is that I think making sure the definition of critical risk is correct is absolutely fundamental.

One of the features of critical risks is that they can often appear infrequently, but when they do in our workplaces, the outcomes are very severe. So including a likelihood definition is problematic, and the risk there is that legitimate critical risks would be declassified, and therefore put workers at harm as a result of that.

The other adjacent point, I think, is around making sure that duty holders do have guidance available for them to understand what that focus on critical risk looks like and feels like in practice. We know that there's inconsistencies in approach there across different industry sectors.

We are very supportive of the industry-led approved codes of practice and associated guidance. One of the things that we'd be keen the committee considers is how will the development and production of that guidance and approved codes of practice be resourced? Making sure that there are funds and resources to enable PCBUs to work on what good looks like by that, and ensuring that it's made available across all industry sectors is, I think, a really important enabling step.

Back to you, Max. Yeah. Well, I think that you will have heard from a number of businesses, but it's the opportunity to ask us some questions at this stage. Thank you very much. I do appreciate that. I'll just see if we have any questions from the committee. Don't take any lack of questions as a lack of interest though, please.

We read all of these submissions, and usually when we don't have questions, it means you've put the submission together very, very well. So with that, I think we might let you go and give you back the rest of your time, and we'll move on to our next submitter. So thank you very much, Max and Rob. Thank you. Thanks.
Appreciate it. Wonderful. We'll move on now to the HS Education Forum. We have Dave Moore.

Welcome, Dave. Hi there, Katie. Hello. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, that includes time for questions, and we'll leave it over to you now. Thank you very much. Thank you, everybody. I think we're going to be joined by a colleague of mine, Mia Aluna, from the forum as well. So we're representing a health and safety education forum, which is facilitated by hazards presently.

Mia is the student representative, and I think Mia is a early career student working full-time in the primary industry. It's a difficult primary industry whilst trying to study and get herself established. So we felt it'd be useful particularly having her here for questions if people have any questions.

I'm at the other end of the career spectrum, as it were. I worked in health and safety as an educator, applied researcher, and a consultant for about 30 years in just about every sector, and worked for a number of the universities, and still am.

So I have a foot in each camp in that sense. I think the education forum is, you'll have read from the submission, but just to quickly recap, I think the crucial thing here is particularly following Pike River, it was clear that across all of the professional bodies that were involved in helping businesses-Come up with solutions.

There's complex problems require complex solutions, and so hazards reflects that aspect of it. The Education, Health and Safety Forum draws on those different bodies, but also people out in various areas of industry that can help us with that larger question of what we need to teach to who, and what kind of delivery is crucial for them.

Why we submitted, the core reason why we submitted is that I feel within that forum, we've got a unique perspective on how the mechanisms within OHS work. I mean, at a government level and in my previous permanent role that I had, I was a manager at WorkSafe for Health and Safety by Design, so I've got some understanding of the difficulties of working at a parliamentary level, making the rules, and then understanding whether you can actually apply these or not, and the costs associated with enforcement. And I think because we do have that

understanding of the mechanisms, we felt it could be a useful contribution to make to this overall discussion. Our overall position on the bill is that we absolutely support the objectives. I think anyone working in this area would be struggling to not do so. I think it's a question of how we achieve them, and I think in my experience, I go back to the '92 act and then obviously later on with the next act.

In both cases, I believe what was implicit about responsibilities and how this is all going to work was never made explicit. Because we'd gone from a system in the '80s where every sector had its own framework to work to, it had its own inspectors, and so that expertise that we're missing now, frankly, we gave that away when we moved to the umbrella.

Which the umbrella approach had benefits, but we also gave away that, and there was always the understanding, clearly with the second act, that we were going to be replacing that expertise with the regulations, the codes of practice and so forth. So that two-tier system, which you see in most of the professions, law, medicine, you have your first principles, underlying first principles, which is a common language, a shared language, and you need literacy of that language across all the stakeholders in order to be able to achieve these

advances. So I think in order to achieve the objectives that are set out here, firstly, we need to be more closely defining costs. Are we talking about simply registered businesses, or are we looking at the society as a whole? Because clearly, if someone's unemployable for the second half of their working life through, say, gradual onset musculoskeletal disorders, that individual, their experience is costing the family and the community because of a loss of ability to contribute, in an unpaid way as well. And I think particularly looking at the

draft as it stands at the moment for the bill, I believe the area that we could end up with increased cost, and unless we're very careful, is gradual onset leading to musculoskeletal disorders. I've done a lot of work with ACC over the years, and the account that always causes the most problem is the ongoing.

The musculoskeletal, it just doesn't go away within a few months. That's the part that they're always pointing us at and saying, "What can we do about this group here?" Unfortunately, because of claim migration, the motivations for people to simplify their claims process by perhaps overemphasizing the acute nature and underrepresenting the gradual onset, that's really problematic from a research point of view because it gives a skewed impression of actually what's going on out in industry. And I think some of the early work that

was done to do with burden of harm by NOSAC a few years ago, that was starting to give ourselves a sense as to what is the real total burden of cost and who's paying it. And I think societally, we carry a huge amount for industries who could do more to make jobs able to be done throughout a working life, particularly those related to musculoskeletal disorders.

So I think the second thing that we need to achieve is a higher level of occupational health and safety literacy, not just across people with that in their job description, because we all have shared responsibilities under the act, including visitors to sites, including the general public. So I think that sense of higher level of literacy, which I know in some countries where this is included into school leaver education and so forth, is a really crucial part of it.

Otherwise, again, we don't achieve that reduction in cost that we're looking for, the overall cost. And I think thirdly, we've need to have that sector-specific expertise and application reflected in the work that's done by the regulators. So the regulations that we effectively gave away in '92, that industry expertise and understanding, that's the part that I think is the most crucial bit.

And while it sounds as though it's going to cost more money up front, I think in the longer term, the savings to New Zealand as a whole are where the real cost benefits can come. In order to achieve that, I suppose the third thing that we'd like to encourage the committee to look hard at is the implementation of this act.

Because at the point that it goes through, there was things said implicitly in both the 2015 and 1992. They were there implicitly, if people wanted to pick them up and enforce them. They weren't explicit enough, and there wasn't the budgets for it. That missing piece, I thinkIs something where this act could make a hell of a difference and actually start to turn that, particularly the ongoing burden of cost from musculoskeletal disorders, could turn that around.

And I think probably I've said as much as I should to leave a bit of time for questions if anyone has any. Great. Yeah, look, I just had one quick question, Dave, just about your... You just touched on it before with the implicit, versus the explicit, and you talk a lot about the training. Who do you see that cost falling onto? Does that become an organizational requirement or is that a pre-job training and industry training requirement, do you think?

I think I see it at two different levels, Katie. On the one hand, we've got the question of first principles, and that training I would say that's coming more underneath education. I would say the explicit element of it sitting in the development of the regulations, codes of practice and so forth. And to an extent, if you look at it simply and say, well, those who benefit should bear the cost.

At the same time, I think, if you take more of a public health model, that field of benefit ripples further and further, into intergenerational benefit, in the same way as when we look at, for example, adult literacy.
